coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of alkylated carbon dots with blue emission in halogenated benzene solvents
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Dandan
Yin, Keyang
Zhao, Ya
Gao, Zelin
Godbert, Nicolas
Li, Hongmei
Li, Hongguang
Hao, Jingcheng
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) has attracted much attention in recent years. In this article, alkylated, blue-emitting CDs are prepared from dodecylamine through pyrolysis in differently substituted benzene solvents. The influences of the solvent on the size, yield and fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs have been investigated in detail. Overall, eight solvents were probed. It was found that the presence of halogen atom(s) in the solvent structure is beneficial for the production of the CDs, as seen from the poor yields of the CDs obtained in methylated benzenes. For the three mono-substituted halogenated benzenes with Cl, Br, I, as the atomic number of the halogen atom increases, hence increasing the resulting polarizability of the medium, the quantity of CDs obtained after pyrolysis also increases. For the two dichlorobenzenes probed, only the ortho-substitution is advantageous for the production of the CDs. The major quantity of CDs was obtained in iodobenzene (∼26 %) while the highest fluorescence quantum yield was observed for CDs prepared in bromobenzene (∼24 %), which is almost 4 times and 3 times that of the CDs developed in the old strategy using chlorobenzene as a solvent. Not only the change of the solvent tunes the photophysical properties of the CDs, it also alters their chemical structure. While the mono-substituted solvent is conducive to CDs with enriched C = O, the di-substituted one facilitates instead the formation of C-O. The possible interaction between the alkylamine and the solvent during pyrolysis was probed by adding lewis base and lewis acid to the system. Evidence of the occurrence of a process similar to Friedel-Crafts alkylation has been found. Our research illustrates new synthetic pathways and mechanism analysis for the preparation of CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Alkylated
Carbon dots
Solvent
Polarizability
Halogen

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Formation of CDs in different solvents
Mechanisms for the formation of the CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Illustration of the preparation of the CDs (for CDs, only the carbon nuclei and alkyl chains are shown, the possible polar functional groups are not drawn). b) Chemical structures of the five benzene solvents used for the preparation of the CDs. c) Photos of the diluted samples (to ∼10 times of the original volume) after pyrolyzing DDA in different solvents, taken under room light (top) and 365 nm UV irradiation (bottom). d) Appearance of CDs1-I in the solvent-free state under room light and 365 nm UV. e) Digital photos of purification of CDs under room light and 365 nm UV irradiation. f) Typical TEM image and corresponding size distribution of CDs1,2-Cl.

a, b) FTIR spectra of the CDs and DDA. The spectra are normalized by the area of the signals from the alkyl chains between 2950 cm−1 and 2860 cm−1. c) 1H NMR spectra of the CDs in CDCl3. d) 13C NMR spectra of the CDs in CDCl3. e, f) High-resolution O1s and N1s spectra for CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br and CDs1,2-Cl.

a) UV–vis absorbance of the CDs in ethanol at 0.01 mg⋅ mL−1. b) Emission of the CDs1,2-Cl at different excitation wavelengths (λex) with a concentration of 5 mg⋅ mL−1. c) Statistics of the PL intensity and corresponding emission wavelengths (λem) for the CDs with a concentration of 5.0 mg⋅ mL−1. Emission were recorded with a λex of 360-420 nm for CDs1-Cl, 390-450 nm for CDs1-Br, 440-500 nm for CDs1-I, 380-440 nm for CDs1,2-Cl and 370-430 nm for CDs1,3-Cl with a step of 10 nm. d) Emission spectra of different CDs (5.0 mg⋅ mL−1) at the optimal λex. e) Contents of N and O of the CDs obtained from elemental analysis. The calculated value of DDA is also given for comparison. f) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves at optimal λex in ethanol (5 mg⋅ mL−1).
a) Photos of CDs obtained by adding DDA, ODB and different additives, taken under roomlight (top) and 365 nm UV irradiation (bottom). b) HR-XPS spectrum of Cl2p for CDs1,2-Cl-Al.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of alkylated carbon dots with blue emission in halogenated benzene solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MAT_INLAB (Laboratorio di Materiali Molecolari Inorganici), Centro di Eccelenza CEMIF.CAL, LASCAMM CR-INSTM della Calabria, Dipartimento di Chimica e Tecnologie Chimiche, Università della Calabria, 87036 Arcavacata di Rende, CS, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dipartimento di Chimica e Tecnologie Chimiche

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MAT_INLAB (Laboratorio di Materiali Molecolari Inorganici), Centro di Eccelenza CEMIF.CAL, LASCAMM CR-INSTM della Calabria, Dipartimento di Chimica e Tecnologie Chimiche, Università della Calabria, 87036 Arcavacata di Rende (CS), Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Polymers, Shandong Academy of Pharmaceutical Science, Jinan, 250100, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Biomedical Polymers, Shandong Academy of Pharmaceutical Science, Jinan, 250100, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alkylated, blue-emitting CDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing dodecylamine (DDA) in substituted benzene solvents. By tuning the structure of the solvent, both the yield and fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs have been greatly improved. Meanwhile, a change of the solvent tunes the structure and property of the CDs.

The synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) has attracted much attention in recent years. In this article, alkylated, blue-emitting CDs are prepared from dodecylamine through pyrolysis in differently substituted benzene solvents. The influences of the solvent on the size, yield and fluorescence quantum yield of the CDs have been investigated in detail. Overall, eight solvents were probed. It was found that the presence of halogen atom(s) in the solvent structure is beneficial for the production of the CDs, as seen from the poor yields of the CDs obtained in methylated benzenes. For the three mono-substituted halogenated benzenes with Cl, Br, I, as the atomic number of the halogen atom increases, hence increasing the resulting polarizability of the medium, the quantity of CDs obtained after pyrolysis also increases. For the two dichlorobenzenes probed, only the ortho-substitution is advantageous for the production of the CDs. The major quantity of CDs was obtained in iodobenzene (∼26 %) while the highest fluorescence quantum yield was observed for CDs prepared in bromobenzene (∼24 %), which is almost 4 times and 3 times that of the CDs developed in the old strategy using chlorobenzene as a solvent. Not only the change of the solvent tunes the photophysical properties of the CDs, it also alters their chemical structure. While the mono-substituted solvent is conducive to CDs with enriched C = O, the di-substituted one facilitates instead the formation of C-O. The possible interaction between the alkylamine and the solvent during pyrolysis was probed by adding lewis base and lewis acid to the system. Evidence of the occurrence of a process similar to Friedel-Crafts alkylation has been found. Our research illustrates new synthetic pathways and mechanism analysis for the preparation of CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanoallotropes are a huge family of functional materials, which can be classified in terms of their dimensionality (0D, 1D, 2D, 3D) or by the hybridization state of the carbon atoms (sp, sp2, sp3) [1]. In 1985, C60 molecule was discovered [2], which was followed by the discovery of carbon nanotubes in 1991 [3] and graphene in 2004 [4]. In recent years, there has been growing interest in carbon-based photoluminescent (PL) nanomaterials, including graphene quantum dots, polymer dots and carbon dots (CDs) [5–7]. Specifically, the research of CDs has been a hot topic. Their discovery can be traced back to a kind of luminous carbon nanomaterials discovered by Xu et al. in 2004 [8]. In 2006, Sun et al. named CDs for this unique type of fluorescence materials [9]. Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots, the newly-discovered CDs display some advantageous performance, including high stability of the photoluminescence, rich sources of raw materials, simple preparation methods, easily modifiable surface and low toxicity [10–15]. They have shown wide applications in the detection of heavy metal ions, bioimaging, catalysis and optoelectronic devices [16–19].

At the early stage, CDs were synthesized mainly by the top-down method where large-sized bulk materials were cut into small-sized CDs by physical or chemical methods [8,20]. Later, a more popular bottom-up method was developed where organic molecules were used as precursors and CDs were obtained by pyrolysis [21,22]. It is well-known that the structure and property of the CDs will be determined by the starting materials and the experimental conditions adopted during pyrolysis [22,23]. As the preparation of the CDs has been expanded from aqueous solutions to nonaqueous ones, influence of the solvents used during pyrolysis has received increasing attention. Song et al. synthesized CDs from p-phenylenediamine through a solvothermal method in five solvents with varying polarity [24]. It was found that the solvent can influence the contents of carbon and nitrogen elements, leading to an obvious variation of the photoluminescent property of the CDs. In another work, they prepared CDs with double emission through solvothermal method in toluene [25]. In contrast, CDs prepared in water only shows blue emission. Qu’s group reported the preparation of full-color emissive CDs from citric acid and urea [26]. By changing the solvent during synthesis, the authors demonstrated how the solvents affect the dehydration and carbonization processes. It can be seen that the type of solvent selected during synthesis plays an important role on determining the structures and properties of CDs.

Recently, we have shown that aliphatic amines can be used as raw materials to prepare alkylated CDs in chlorobenzene (CB) [27,28]. This discovery shows that organic compounds with only one functional group can be also converted into CDs under mild condition. To further optimize the synthetic pathway of this new class of CDs, in this work we focus on the influences of different solvents on the synthesis of CDs. Dodecylamine (DDA) was selected as a starting material and its pyrolysis in eight benzene solvents was investigated in detail. It was found that the production of the CDs has an obvious correlation with the polarizability of the solvent. If the solvent has a higher polarizability, more CDs will be obtained as seen from the samples using bromobenzene, iodobenzene and 1,2-dichlorobenzene as solvents. In addition, the structures and properties of the CDs also were tuned by the solvents. Meanwhile, possible interaction between the solvent molecule and DDA was explored by adding lewis base or lewis acid to the system.

Dodecylamine (DDA, 98 %), chlorobenzene (CB, analytical grade), bromobenzene (BB, analytical grade), iodobenzene (IB, analytical grade), 1,2-dichlorobenzene (ODB, analytical grade), 1,3-dichlorobenzene (MDB, analytical grade), 1,2-xylene (OX, analytical grade), 1,3-xylene (MX, analytical grade), 1,4-xylene (PX, analytical grade), potassium carbonate (K2CO3, 99 %) and aluminum chloride (AlCl3, 99 %) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ethanol and n-hexane were obtained from local supplier with the quality of analytical grade. All chemicals were used without further purification.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a JEM1400 instrument operating at 120 kV. For sample preparation, a drop of sample solution (∼20 μL) was placed on a holey-carbon coated copper grid (300 meshes) and dried by an infrared lamp. Fluorescence spectra of the solution of CDs were obtained by a spectrofluorometer (FluoroMax-4). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a spectrometer (Tensor II) using KBr pellets. UV–vis measurements were carried out on a UV–vis-NIR spectrometer (cary 5000). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out on a TGA 5500 and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) measurements were carried out on a DSC250 under nitrogen with a scanning speed of 10 °C min−1. The absolute fluorescence quantum yields and fluorescence lifetimes were measured with a spectrofluorometer (FLSP920, Edinburgh Instruments LTD). X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (ESCALAB 250) with a monochromatized Al Kα X-ray source (1486.71 eV). Before measurements, the samples were spin coated on silicon substrate. Elemental analysis was carried out with Vario EL CUBE (Elementar).

The alkylated CDs were synthesized by a pyrolysis method established by us recently [27,28]. In a typical experiment, desired amount of DDA was dissolved in 50 mL solvent in a three-necked flask with a volume of 100 mL. The mixture was heated for 48 h under magnetic stirring. The concentration of DDA was fixed at 1.0 mol⋅L−1 and the temperatures were set according to the boiling points of different series of solvents, which are 150 °C for xylene, 185 °C for dichlorobenzene, and 140 °C for mono-substituted solvents including CB, BB, IB. After the pyrolysis, the mixture was naturally cooled to room temperature. The as-prepared mixture was checked by thin layer chromatography (TLC) to find suitable solvent system for subsequent purification by silica gel chromatography. Hexane was selected first as the eluent where the target fluorescent CDs show a very low Rf value (Rf ≈0, Fig. S1). Under this condition, the solvent with high boiling point used during pyrolysis can be facilely removed by the column. After that, the eluent was changed to ethanol where the fluorescent CDs show a high Rf value approaching 1 while the starting material exhibits a much smaller Rf value, as demonstrated by our previous study [27]. The progress of the purification on the column can be monitored with the aid of an ultraviolet lamp. The section containing CDs was collected and ethanol was removed under reduced pressure. The products were further dried in vacuum before characterizations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formation of CDs in different solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation route of the CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 a. DDA was used as the starting material and the pyrolysis was carried out in different solvents (Fig. 1b). First, phenomena in mono-substituted solvents (CB, BB, IB) were checked. It is known that DDA can form CDs in CB by being heated at 140 °C for 48 h [27]. The diluted sample only shows a weak yellow color. When CB was replaced by BB, the sample shows an obvious yellow color. When BB was further changed to IB, the color of the sample becomes even heavier. As DDA is colorless, this accentuated color change is indicative of the increased extent of pyrolysis and the improved production of CDs.

The pyrolysis of DDA in di-substituted solvents (ODB, MDB, xylene) were also examined. Samples in ODB were prepared at two temperatures which are 140 °C and 185 °C, respectively. In both cases, the samples show colors heavier than that of the sample prepared in CB (Fig. S2). Pyrolysis in MDB was found to be less effective, but the diluted sample is also yellowish (Fig. 1c). When xylene was tried, however, the samples are almost colorless (Fig. S2) no matter how the two substituted methyl groups are arranged on the benzene ring, which indicates a low extent of pyrolysis. This implies that halogen atom has a significant influence in the carbonization process.

Normally, chemical reactions involved in the production of CDs are complicated, and to reveal the detailed mechanism of pyrolysis is a big task. Nevertheless, it was found that the performance of the solvents during pyrolysis is closely related to their polarizability. For the three mono-substituted solvents, the polarizability (α) follows the order of CB (12.25) < BB (13.62) < IB (15.52) [29]. Thus, it turns out that a bigger α facilitates the pyrolysis of DDA. This rule can be also applied to di-substituted solvents. For ODB, the polarizability is larger than CB due to the presence of the additional chlorine on the benzene ring. The lower ability of MDB in the promotion of the pyrolysis can be also explained by its decreased α compared to ODB [29]. Finally, the worst performance of xylene can be understood by their lowest α among all the tested solvents.

Through purification, the five purified CDs were denoted according to the solvent used, i.e., CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br, CDs1-I, CDs1,2-Cl and CDs1,3-Cl. The yield (Y) of each type of CDs was calculated based on the ratio of the weight of the purified CDs to that of initially-added DDA. The highest Y was found for CDs1-I (25.60 %), which is followed by CDs1-Br (24.90 %), CDs1,2-Cl (21.52 %), CDs1,3-Cl (15.30 %) and CDs1-Cl (7.08 %). The result of Y is fully consistent with the visual observations on the diluted samples before purification as shown in Fig. 1c. From these results, one can see that by carefully selecting the solvent used during the pyrolysis, the pyrolysis of DDA can be greatly promoted. Importantly, all the four newly-tested solvents showed better performance than previously-tested CB. In addition, a change in solvent not only improves the Y of the CDs, but also tunes their structures and properties, as will be described in detail in the following sections.

The purified CDs were obtained as room-temperature fluids. While CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br and CDs1-I show relatively low viscosity and can flow under the gravity, CDs1,2-Cl and CDs1,3-Cl are highly sticky and can hardly flow. Fig. 1d gives the photos of CDs1-I as an example, while Fig. 1e gives photos of the five purified CDs in solvent-free state under room light and 365 nm UV irradiation, respectively. For CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br and CDs1-I, the color becomes increasingly heavier. CDs1-Cl is light yellow and shows blue fluorescence. CDs1-Br is light orange and shows weak green fluorescence. CDs1-I is dark orange and undergoes self-quenching. The increased absorbance in the visible region of these three CDs indicates that the extent of the pyrolysis of DDA becomes successively heavier. CDs1,2-Cl and CDs1,3-Cl look similar with CDs1-Br under room light. They show greenish and white fluorescence under UV, respectively. The five CDs show similar thermal stability as proved by their TGA curves (Fig. S3). The 1% weight loss appears at ∼117 °C and the major weight loss occurs before 443 °C (up to ∼97.5 % for CDs1-Cl, CDs1-I, CDs1,2-Cl and ∼93.6 % for CDs1-Br, CDs1,3-Cl). From DSC curves, CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br, CDs1-I and CDs1,3-Cl show m p below or around 0 °C (Fig. S4).

The morphologies of the CDs were checked by TEM observations. All the CDs appear as well-dispersed, quasi-spherical particles. A typical image of CDs1,2-Cl is shown in Fig. 2 f and the results for other CDs are given in Fig. S5. The average diameters for CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br and CDs1-I were determined to be 2.42 nm, 3.06 nm and 3.25 nm, respectively. The continuous increase in size is consistent with the increased degree of carbonization of DDA in this series of solvents. The average diameters of CDs1,2-Cl and CDs1,3-Cl were found to be 2.62 nm and 2.52 nm, respectively.

The surface groups of the CDs were identified by FTIR studies, as summarized in Fig. 2a, b. The spectra were normalized by the area of the signals from the alkyl chains between 2950 and 2860 cm−1. For DDA, the stretching vibration of NH locates at 3336 cm-1 [9,30], which shifts to 3329, 3310, 3323 and 3323 cm−1 in CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br, CDs1,2-Cl and CDs1,3-Cl, accompanying a decrease in the peak intensity. For CDs1-I, the signal from NH almost diminishes totally. These observations suggest that the amino group on DDA participated in the pyrolysis to become a part of the functional group on the surface of the CDs. The vibrations of CO at 1690∼1670 cm−1 are wider and sharper in CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br and CDs1-I compared to CDs1,2-Cl and CDs1.3-Cl, revealing that the former contains more CO [18,31]. Combined with elemental analysis (Table S2), CDs1,2-Cl and CDs1,3-Cl have higher oxygen content, but they have lower contents of CO proved by FTIR. This shows that oxygen is in the form of CO in these two CDs. Signals at 1078∼1030 cm−1, which are assigned to the vibrations of CN/CO, also confirmed this [32,33]. In addition, peaks at 1600∼1500 cm−1 were observed especially for CDs1-Br and CDs1,2-Cl, which is indicative of the formation of amide bonds [34].

NMR measurements were carried out and the spectra were normalized by signals from the CH3 group (Fig. 2c, d). Signals from protons on sp3 hybridized carbons were mainly observed at1.60∼0.85 ppm in 1H NMR spectra [35], with some exceptions appearing at 3∼2.3 ppm. This chemical shift toward low field can be ascribed to the decreased electron density of these protons caused by the presence of the electron withdrawing groups such as the benzene ring or carbonyl, which reduces the shielding effect on the magnetic field [36,37]. Meanwhile, the high density of sharp signals shows that alkyl chains contain various chain isomers of the repetitive unit [38]. Signals in the range of 4∼1.5 ppm and 8∼7 ppm correspond to amide and aromatic protons, respectively [31]. Signals between 9.5∼9.0 ppm reveal the presence of aldehyde groups, which are more obvious for CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br and CDs1-I compared to CDs1,2-Cl and CDs1,3-Cl. This implies that there are more aldehyde groups in the CDs prepared by mono-substituent solvents. In 13C NMR spectra, signals in the range of 42∼23 ppm, 160∼110 ppm and 170∼200 ppm correspond to sp3 hybridized carbons, sp2 carbons and the carbonyl/amide groups of the CDs, respectively [21,39]. CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br and CDs1-I have stronger resonance absorption peak signal at ∼190 ppm from the carbonyl groups [38], which is consistent with the results of 1H NMR spectra.

To get further structural details of the CDs, XPS measurements were carried out on three representative CDs, i.e., CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br and CDs1,2-Cl. As described in Fig. 2e, f and Figs. S7–9, the results show that the CDs contain carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, which is consistent with the results from elemental analysis. The high-resolution O1s spectra reveal two main peaks at 531.8 eV and 532.3 eV, corresponding to CO and CO [39]. In addition, a small amount of external Si and O (in the form of Si-O) were also detected in the spectra, which originated from the silicon substrate. From high-resolution O1s spectra, CDs1,2-Cl contains more CO while CDs1-Br has more CO, which is consistent with the results from FTIR. From high-resolution N1s spectra, the peaks at 401.5∼400.6 eV can be assigned to pyrrolic N and those at 399.8∼399.5 eV belong to pyridinic N and amino N. Pyridinic N can contribute to π-conjugated system with a pair of p-electrons [40]. The deconvoluted spectra of N1s reveal that CDs1,2-Cl has more pyridinic N and amino N.

The optical properties of the CDs in solutions were evaluated in detail. From UV–vis absorption measurements shown in Fig. 3 a, CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br and CDs1-I have two conspicuous absorption peaks around 224 nm and 280 nm, which can be attributed to π-π* transition of the unsaturated aromatic ring and n-π* transition of the C = O or C-N, respectively [10,37]. For CDs1,2-Cl and CDs1,3-Cl, these two peaks become blurry. CDs1,2-Cl has a stronger absorption compared to CDs1,3-Cl, which is consistent with visual observations. From steady-state fluorescence measurements, the excitation spectrum of each type of CDs partially overlaps with its UV–vis absorption (Fig. S10), meaning that emission is related to the lower-energy region [39]. All the CDs exhibit blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation when dissolved in ethanol. Wavelength-dependent emission was observed (Fig. 3b, Fig. S11), which implies the existence of multiple emissive centers in the CDs. For CDs1-Cl, CDs1-Br and CDs1-I, a continuous red shift of the emission was observed. At a concentration of 5.0 mg⋅ mL−1, the strongest emission was recorded to be 470 nm for CDs1-Cl, 490 nm for CDs1-Br and 540 nm for CDs1-I, respectively (Fig. 3c). This red shift can be partially ascribed to the increased size of the CDs as proved by TEM [41]. The strongest emission for CDs1,2-Cl and CDs1,3-Cl with a concentration of 5.0 mg⋅ mL-1 occurs at 487 nm and 480 nm, respectively. Influence of concentration on the emission of the CDs was also investigated at the optimal excitations of the CDs (Fig. S12). The strongest emission was obtained at 3.0 mg⋅ mL−1 for CDs1-Br and CDs1,3-Cl, and 5.0 mg⋅ mL−1 for CDs1-I and CDs1,2-Cl. For CDs1-Cl, it has not reached the strongest emission within the investigated concentration range (up to 7.0 mg⋅ mL−1). Although the fluorescence intensity is related to the concentration for each type of the CDs, the position of the emission peak only changes slightly. This shows that the CDs can emit strong fluorescence at low concentrations. The luminescence stability of the CDs under continuous UV irradiation has been tested by using CDs1,2-Cl aqueous solution as a typical example following the method reported in the literature [42]. Under long time UV irradiation up to 95 min, no obvious decrease of the PL intensity was observed (Fig. S13), confirming the high luminescence stability of CDs.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the CDs were measured in DMF for better comparison with our previous results [27]. The QY follows the order of CDs1-Br > CDs1,2-Cl > CDs1-Cl > CDs1,3-Cl > CDs1-I, which is 23.54 %, 14.13 %, 8.78 %, 7.77 % and 7.26 %, respectively. Compared with other CDs prepared in non-aqueous systems, the CDs reported in current work have relatively higher QY (Table S1). Importantly, the QY of CDs1-Br (23.54 %) is significantly higher than that of CDs1-Cl (8.78 %), indicating that the condition for the pyrolysis of DDA has been optimized. This progress, together with the significantly improved Y of the CDs, is quite meaningful when pushing these alkylated CDs into practical applications.

The higher QY obtained for CDs1-Br and CDs1,2-Cl could be attributed to their higher contents of both oxygen and nitrogen (Fig. 3e, Table S2), which is consistent with the proposed mechanism where the emission of CDs is controlled by surface functional groups [30,34]. Under the optimal excitation wavelength, the average lifetimes were determined by time-resolved fluorescence measurements to be in a range of 4.98 ∼ 6.04 ns. And in all the cases, the decay spectra could be fitted by bi-exponential functions (Fig. 3f and Table S3), which correspond to the recombination from the intrinsic state (π−π*) and the defect states (n−π*), respectively [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms for the formation of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the stories happened during the pyrolysis of DDA, next we commit to understand the role of solvents in the formation of CDs. As the purified CDs do not contain halogen, we first considered the possibility of the involvement of the solvent fragments in the final product through intermediated steps of intermolecular substitution (Scheme S1a, taking ODB for an example). If this is the case, hydrogen chloride will be generated and the reaction should be promoted by the addition of a lewis base such as K2CO3. However, the mixture after the reaction is almost colorless (Fig. 4 a), indicating that the formation of the CDs was hindered. The lower Y observed in an environment with higher basicity is reminiscent of our previous result where Y of the CDs decreases at higher concentration of DDA [27]. These results indicate that even these intermediated steps do exist during pyrolysis, they are not the key to promote the formation of the CDs.

Contrary to the effect of the base, we found that the addition of a lewis acid such as AlCl3 can greatly improve the formation of the CDs. In this case, ODB was also selected as a model solvent. The color of the mixture changed significantly and finally became almost black (Fig. 4a). The weight ratio of the purified CDs (denoted as CDs1,2-Cl-Al) to the initially-added DDA reached 125 %, indicating that other components must be involved during the formation of CDs. QY was determined to be 5.92 %. The high-resolution spectrum of Cl2p can be also deconvoluted into three peaks at 199.15 eV, 197.87 eV and 197.12 eV, which are assigned to Cl2p3/2, C-Cl and Cl-Al-O, respectively (Fig. 4b) [43]. The existence of C-Cl could be ascribed to two possible reasons. One is the carbonization of DDA together with Cl− from AlCl3. The other is the involvement of the solvent molecule (i.e., ODB) or its debris in CDs1,2-Cl-Al. In the latter case, a mechanism similar to Friedel-Crafts alkylation could be envisaged (Scheme S1b) [44,45]. When AlCl3 is added, it accelerates the formation of C12H25 + and promotes the interaction between ODB and DDA. This can explain why CDs1,2-Cl does not have halogen in XPS whereas CDs1,2-Cl-Al has C-Cl in high-resolution spectrum of Cl2p. Based on these observations, it can be concluded that the solvent molecule does not merely provides an environment for the pyrolysis of DDA. Instead, it might also contribute to the formation of the CDs.

It should be emphasized that the interaction between the solvent and DDA only contributes partially to the whole process of the pyrolysis. As a typical bottom-up method, pyrolysis will trigger the condensation between the active functional groups of the staring material (DDA in current study), which is followed by carbonization at higher temperatures or prolonged heating. In addition, oxidation of the reactants will occur by the oxygen in the air or trapped in the system. These processes are generally accepted to explain the formation of CDs in most systems, both in aqueous and nonaqueous environments. In our studies, these processes are also believed the dominant pathways for the formation of the CDs, although the interaction between the solvent molecule and DDA should also be taken into consideration.

In summary, alkylated and blue-emitting CDs have been successfully prepared from alkylamine in five benzene solvents with different molecular structures. It was found that the structure of the solvent can obviously influence the Y and QY of the CDs, and the solvent with a higher α facilitates a deeper carbonization. For the three solvents mono-substituted by Cl, Br and I, Y of the CDs increases with increasing atomic number of the halogen atom. For the two solvents di-substituted with Cl, the one with ortho-substitution is advantageous for the production of the CDs. The highest Y was found in IB (25.60 %) and the highest QY was obtained in BB (23.54 %), which is ∼3.6 times and ∼2.7 times that of the CDs developed in the old strategy using CB as a solvent. Thus, by carefully screening the solvent, both Y and QY of the alkylated CDs can be significantly improved. A change of the solvent tunes the structure and property of the CDs. While the mono-substituted solvent is conducive to the formation of C = O, the di-substituted one facilitates the formation of CO. The possible interaction between the alkylamine and the solvent during pyrolysis was probed by adding lewis base and lewis acid to the system. Evidence of the occurrence of a process similar to Friedel-Crafts alkylation has been found. The success in improving both Y and QY of the alkylated CDs would greatly push these interesting carbon nanomaterials into practical applications, while the deep understanding of the mechanism behind the pyrolysis would be beneficial for rational design of new CDs in future.

Dandan Lu: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Keyang Yin: Investigation, Data curation. Ya Zhao: Investigation. Zelin Gao: Software. Nicolas Godbert: Data curation. Hongmei Li: Resources. Hongguang Li: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing. Jingcheng Hao: Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21875129), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2018ZA0547) and the Fundamental Research Funds of Shandong University (No. 2018TB033) are greatly acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2020.126129.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Broad family of carbon nanoallotropes: classification, chemistry, and applications of fullerenes, carbon dots, nanotubes, graphene, Nanodiamonds, and combined superstructures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Georgakilas, J. A. Perman, J. Tucek, R. Zboril, Broad Family of Carbon Nanoallotropes: Classification, Chemistry, and Applications of Fullerenes, Carbon Dots, Nanotubes, Graphene, Nanodiamonds, and Combined Superstructures, Chem. Rev. 115 (2015) 4744-4822.
H. W. Kroto, J. R. Heath, S. C. O’Brien, R. F. Curl, R. E. Smalley, C60: Buckminsterfullerene. Nature. 318 (1985) 162-163.
S. Iijima, Helical Microtubules of Graphitic Carbon, Nature 354(1991), 56-58.
